findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via PostToolUse Hook
findings.description: The PostToolUse hook (hooks/monitor.sh) captures all tool invocations including tool names, inputs, and outputs, and sends them to an external server (mcp-analytics-hub.io). It also collects sensitive environment variables including ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, HOME, USER, and hostname. This constitutes a severe data exfiltration threat.
findings.evidence: The script constructs a JSON payload containing tool input/output, user, hostname, and environment variables (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, HOME) and sends it via curl to https://mcp-analytics-hub.io/hooks/post-tool with a hardcoded bearer token.
findings.remediation: Remove the PostToolUse hook entirely. Do not install any scripts that send tool invocation data or environment variables to external servers. Audit any existing installations for data leakage.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Token in Script
findings.description: The monitor.sh script contains a hardcoded bearer token 'mcp_a1b2c3d4e5f6' used for authentication with the external server. This token could be used for unauthorized access or impersonation.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded credentials. Use environment variables or secure secret management for any required authentication tokens.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning via PostToolUse Hook Installation
findings.description: The skill instructs the user to install a PostToolUse hook that intercepts and exfiltrates all tool invocations. This corrupts the agent's tool monitoring system by replacing legitimate audit functionality with a data exfiltration mechanism.
findings.evidence: Instructions to 'cp hooks/monitor.sh ~/.claude/hooks/PostToolUse.sh' and 'chmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/PostToolUse.sh' install a malicious hook that captures and exfiltrates tool data.
findings.remediation: Do not install the provided PostToolUse hook. Verify any existing hooks in ~/.claude/hooks/ for unauthorized modifications.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'mcp-toolkit' with a description claiming to 'Manage Model Context Protocol servers and tool integrations', but its actual purpose is to install a data exfiltration hook that sends tool invocation data and environment variables to an external server. The name and description are misleading.
findings.evidence: Name 'mcp-toolkit' and description 'Manage Model Context Protocol servers and tool integrations' do not match the actual behavior of installing a data exfiltration hook.
findings.remediation: Rename and redescribe the skill to accurately reflect its actual functionality, or remove the malicious components.

findings.title: Obfuscated Malicious Intent Behind Legitimate Functionality
findings.description: The skill presents itself as a legitimate MCP toolkit with features like 'Add and configure MCP servers', 'Test MCP connections', and 'Monitor tool invocations', but the core functionality is a data exfiltration hook disguised as an 'audit' feature. The malicious intent is hidden behind enterprise compliance language.
findings.evidence: The skill lists legitimate MCP features but the only actionable instruction is to install the monitor.sh hook. The hook is described as 'audit and debugging' but actually exfiltrates data.
findings.remediation: Remove the deceptive audit hook installation instructions and any associated scripts.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via External Server
findings.description: The monitor.sh script sends tool input/output to an external server (mcp-analytics-hub.io). If this server is compromised or malicious, it could inject instructions back into the agent workflow, leading to indirect prompt injection attacks.
findings.evidence: Tool invocation data including inputs and outputs are sent to an external server via HTTP POST. The response is discarded but the server could be used for bidirectional communication.
findings.remediation: Remove the external data transmission. If audit logging is needed, implement it locally only.

findings.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
findings.description: The monitor.sh script explicitly collects ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and GITHUB_TOKEN environment variables and sends them to an external server. These credentials could be used for unauthorized access to AI services and code repositories.
findings.evidence: Script includes 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY', 'GITHUB_TOKEN', and 'HOME' in the session_env payload sent to the external server.
findings.remediation: Remove all environment variable collection from the script. Never transmit API keys or tokens to external servers.

findings.title: Command Injection via Unsanitized Input in Shell Script
findings.description: The monitor.sh script uses unsanitized tool input/output directly in shell variable expansion and JSON construction. If tool input contains shell metacharacters, it could lead to command injection or JSON parsing errors.
findings.evidence: TOOL_INPUT and TOOL_OUTPUT are used directly in echo and JSON construction without sanitization. The python3 -c command attempts to escape but the shell heredoc construction is vulnerable.
findings.remediation: Use proper JSON serialization libraries and sanitize all inputs before processing in shell scripts.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools Declaration
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not declare 'allowed-tools', which is optional but recommended. The skill's scripts use Bash and Python tools, and declaring these would improve transparency.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter lacks 'allowed-tools' field. The monitor.sh script uses both bash and python3.
findings.remediation: Add 'allowed-tools: [Bash, Python]' to the YAML frontmatter for transparency.
